subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that telling time works the same way every day.
Tell and write time.
Your child understands what morning, afternoon, day, and night mean.
Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.
Your child understands what tomorrow, yesterday, and today mean.
Your child can read a calendar to tell yesterday, today, and tomorrow and can read and write dates.
Work with time and money.
Your child can tell and write time to the half hour using both clocks with hands and digital clocks.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Time — Telling Time and Understanding Calendar Concepts
coherence_reason: The cluster contains both telling time using clocks and understanding calendar concepts (days, dates), which are related but distinct subtopics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.
Model with mathematics.
Your child can use speaking and body language to share and understand information.
Your child will write their opinion and give reasons to support it.
Your child can use different ways to improve how they talk and share ideas.
Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.
Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.
Your child can use basic reading skills to read stories smoothly and understand what they mean.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Mathematical Practices — Problem Solving and Communication
coherence_reason: The standards include both mathematical problem solving/modeling and general communication/reading skills, indicating different topics.
split_topics: Mathematical Practices: Modeling and Problem Solving

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can count coins by twos, fives, and tens to find out how much money a group of pennies, nickels, and dimes is worth.
Your child can name pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters and know how much each is worth.
Your child can find the total value of pennies, nickels, and dimes up to one dollar, and bills up to $100, using the correct symbols.
Your child can find the total value of groups of one-, five-, or ten-dollar bills up to $100.
Your child can identify pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters, say their values using the ¢ symbol, and know how many make a dollar.
Your child can find the total value of groups of pennies, nickels, or dimes up to one dollar.
Your child can count a mix of dimes and pennies and find out how many cents they are worth, up to 100 cents.
Your child can recognize pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters, know their values, and use the cent symbol (¢) correctly.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Money — Identifying Coins and Determining Value
coherence_reason: All standards focus on identifying coins, knowing their values, and counting groups of coins and bills to find total value.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare lengths by using a third object.
Your child can compare objects to see which is longer, shorter, taller, or smaller.
Your child can measure and compare the length of three objects using either standard or everyday items as units.
Your child can compare and put in order the lengths of up to three objects by directly looking at them.
Your child can compare and put in order the lengths of up to three objects by measuring or comparing directly.
Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare two objects by using a third object.
Your child can pick three objects that are different lengths.
Your child can place two groups of objects side by side to compare them.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Measurement — Comparing and Ordering Lengths
coherence_reason: All standards focus on directly comparing and ordering the lengths of up to three objects using measurement or observation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can measure lengths by using repeated units and by comparing lengths indirectly.
Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller objects end to end without gaps or overlaps and counting how many fit.
Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller objects of the same size without gaps or overlaps and count how many it takes.
Your child can measure lengths by using smaller units repeatedly or by measuring indirectly.
Your child can use a ruler to measure how long an object is in whole inches.
Your child can guess the length of an object to the nearest inch and measure it using inches or centimeters.
Your child understands how to use familiar objects to estimate how long an inch is.
Your child can measure how long something is by lining up same-size units without gaps or overlaps and say the length as a whole number.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Measurement — Length Using Non-Standard and Standard Units
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on measuring length using non-standard units, but several include use of standard units like inches and centimeters, as well as estimation.
split_topics: Measuring length with standard units (inches, centimeters) and estimation

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.
Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.
Attend to precision.
Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.
Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.
Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated ideas when solving math problems.
Your child can find and use patterns or structures to help solve math problems.
Your child looks for and explains patterns when solving problems.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Problem Solving — Patterns and Structure
coherence_reason: All standards focus on recognizing, using, and explaining patterns or structure to solve math problems.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into halves and quarters, use words like halves and fourths to describe them, and understand that more pieces mean smaller parts.
Your child learns about shapes by looking at their parts and how they fit together, and practices dividing shapes to understand fractions.
Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are.
Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves, fourths, and quarters to describe them, understanding that more parts mean smaller pieces.
Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and say the area of each part as a fraction of the whole shape.
Your child can divide circles and rectangles into equal parts like halves and thirds, describe these parts, and understand that equal parts can look different.
Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and name these parts as halves or fourths.
Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and call them halves or fourths.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Fractions — Partitioning Shapes into Equal Parts
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on partitioning shapes into equal parts and using fraction language, but one standard about dividing rectangles into rows and columns of squares is more about arrays than fractions.
split_topics: Partitioning rectangles into arrays (rows and columns)

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can organize and understand data with up to three groups, and ask and answer questions about how many items are in each group and compare the groups.
Represent and interpret data.
Your child will write a short report by naming a topic, giving details about it, and finishing with a closing sentence.
Your child uses basic reading skills to read and understand books that give information.
Your child can tell when two groups have the same number of items.
Your child can solve problems by putting two groups of things together.
Your child can compare two groups with up to 10 items each when the groups are close in size.
Your child knows who the leaders are in your town and state and what they do.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Data — Organizing and Comparing Groups
coherence_reason: Most standards are about organizing, representing, and comparing data sets, but a few are unrelated (writing, reading, civics), indicating two distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about shapes and their important features.

Your child can tell the difference between important shape features like having three sides for triangles and less important features like color or size, and can make shapes with the important features.
Your child can sort shapes of the same size and direction, such as circles, squares, rectangles, and triangles.
Your child can tell if something is on, off, in, or out of another object.
Your child uses tools to identify, describe, and draw special four-sided shapes and sorts flat shapes based on their features.
Your child uses tools to explore the features of triangles and circles.
Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.
Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes by folding them to see if the parts match.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Geometry — Shape Attributes and Classification
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on identifying, describing, and classifying shapes by attributes, but some also introduce spatial relations and symmetry, which are related but slightly distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can put two pieces together to make a whole shape, like two half-circles to make a circle.
Your child can put together flat shapes or solid shapes to make new shapes and then create even more shapes from those.
Your child can put together flat or solid shapes to make bigger shapes and then create new shapes from those.
Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into simpler shapes.
Your child can make flat shapes like triangles, rectangles, and squares when given their special features.
Your child understands that bigger shapes can be made by joining smaller shapes and smaller shapes can come from breaking apart bigger ones.
Your child can put together and take apart simple 2D and 3D shapes like triangles, squares, cubes, and cones.
Your child can put together flat shapes like squares and triangles or solid shapes like cubes and cones to make new shapes.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Geometry — Composing and Decomposing Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on composing and decomposing 2D and 3D shapes, but one (about finding area by breaking shapes apart) introduces a slightly advanced sub-concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can tell which activities happen before, next, and after others.
Your child can give examples of how people and families use and change their environment.
Your child can explore, create, and predict repeating patterns made from up to three parts, like shapes or numbers.
Your child can find, describe, continue, and make repeating, growing, and shrinking patterns seen in everyday life.
Your child can find and create growing and repeating patterns with 1s, 5s, and 10s.
Your child can find, describe, and make patterns that grow, shrink, or repeat by adding or subtracting 1s, 2s, 5s, or 10s.
Your child can recognize repeated actions, shapes, or numbers.
Your child learns how traits are passed from parents to offspring and how these traits can vary.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Patterns — Repeating, Growing, and Shrinking Patterns
coherence_reason: Most standards describe various aspects of patterns in math, but two entries are unrelated: one about sequencing events and another about environment and heredity.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Understand place value.
Your child understands that the two digits in a two-digit number show how many tens and ones there are.
Your child understands that 10 is a group of ten ones, called a 'ten.'
Your child can make groups of 10 items.
Your child knows that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten and some extra ones.
Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, and so on mean one, two, three, etc. groups of ten and zero ones.
Your child understands that 10 is a group of ten ones bundled together called a 'ten'.
Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, up to 90 mean one to nine groups of ten and zero ones.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Place Value — Tens and Ones
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding place value in terms of tens and ones, specifically how two-digit numbers are composed.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at their tens and ones digits and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
Your child can compare two numbers up to 100 and use >, <, or = to show which is bigger, smaller, or if they are equal.
Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at their tens and ones and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
Your child can put numbers up to 20 in order and compare which is bigger or smaller.
Your child can place, order, and compare whole numbers up to 100.
Your child can understand and use tens and ones to show numbers and compare two-digit numbers.
Your child can match the > symbol with the words 'greater than' or 'more than.'
Your child can compare two two-digit numbers using >, =, or < symbols.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Number and Operations — Compare and Order Numbers
coherence_reason: All standards focus on comparing and ordering numbers using symbols like >, <, and =, with slight variations in number range and emphasis on ordering versus comparison.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands what the equal sign means and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.
Your child understands that the equal sign means 'the same as' and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.
Your child can decide if simple addition or subtraction sentences with up to three numbers are true or false.
Your child can decide and explain if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.
Your child knows that an equation is a number sentence with symbols like = that shows a math problem.
Engage with the sign for +.
AND/OR
Your child can decide if a math statement with numbers or objects is true or false.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Equations — Understanding the Equal Sign and True/False Statements
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on interpreting the equal sign and evaluating equations as true or false, though a couple of statements are more general or include extraneous details about symbols and basic operations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than any two-digit number in their head and explain how they figured it out.
Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than any two-digit number and explain how they know.
Your child can find the number that is one more, one less, ten more, or ten less than a given two-digit number.
Your child can find the number that is one more and one less than a number up to 20.
Engage with the number ten.
Your child can say the answer when adding 10 to a number up to 100 in their head.
Your child can say the answer when subtracting 10 from a number up to 100 in their head.
Your child can explain how they think about adding ten to a number to find the answer.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Numbers and Operations — Adding and Subtracting 1 and 10
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on mentally finding 1 or 10 more or less than a given number, with slight variation in number range and mention of number sense regarding ten.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 from each other using drawings or strategies based on place value, explain their method, and connect it to written subtraction.
Your child can subtract multiples of 10 up to 100 using drawings, place value ideas, or addition and subtraction facts, and explain how they solved it.
Your child can practice subtracting a one-digit number from numbers between 11 and 19.
Your child can practice subtracting a one-digit number from a two-digit number.
Your child can subtract multiples of 10 from other multiples of 10 between 10 and 90 using drawings, place value, or addition and subtraction strategies, and explain how they did it.
Your child understands that the minus sign (-) means taking away and can show this with models.
Your child practices subtraction facts.
Your child can count backward on a number line to subtract numbers starting from any number.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Subtraction — Basic Facts, Strategies, and Place Value
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on foundational subtraction concepts, including subtracting one-digit numbers and multiples of 10, using place value, models, and explanation, though some focus on facts practice and understanding notation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can count by ones up to 30.
Your child can count to 120 starting from any number below 120, read and write numbers in this range, and match numbers to groups of objects.
Extend the counting sequence.
Your child can count up to 10 objects and write the matching number.
Your child can count to 120, read and write numbers in this range, and match numbers to groups of objects.
Your child can count forward by ones up to 100 and backward by ones up to 20, and count by fives up to 100.
Your child can count forward and backward within 120 by ones, and skip count by 2s to 20 and by 5s to 100.
Your child can read and write numbers from 0 to 100 in normal form, expanded form, and words.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Numbers to 120 — Counting, Reading, and Writing Numbers
coherence_reason: All standards focus on numbers to 120, with most emphasizing counting, some including reading/writing numbers, and some skip counting or number forms; minor variation in emphasis but strongly related.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how to use place value and math rules to add and subtract numbers.
Your child understands and uses math rules and the connection between addition and subtraction.
Your child uses math rules to help add and subtract.
Your child understands and uses the chain rule to find derivatives of combined functions.
Your child understands and uses the rules for adding and subtracting, and knows how they are connected.
Your child can use addition rules to find the total when adding three or more numbers.
Your child understands and uses how addition and subtraction work and how they relate to each other.
Your child uses properties of addition and subtraction to help solve problems.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Properties and Relationships
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on properties and relationships of addition and subtraction, but one standard about the chain rule for derivatives is clearly unrelated and should be split.
split_topics: Addition and subtraction properties and relationships

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding a two-digit number with a one-digit number or a multiple of 10, using drawings or objects and explaining their thinking.
Your child can use objects or pictures to show what number you get when you add one more.
Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding two-digit and one-digit numbers or multiples of 10, using drawings or place value ideas and explain their thinking.
Your child can practice adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number with totals up to 100.
Your child can break apart and put together two-digit numbers using tens and ones with objects, drawings, or number sentences.
Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number or a multiple of 10, using drawings or place value strategies, and explain their thinking.
Your child can use strategies like place value or breaking numbers apart to add a two-digit number and a one-digit number with a total less than 100.
Your child can add ten objects to a number and count up one by one.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Addition — Two-Digit and One-Digit Numbers Using Place Value
coherence_reason: All standards focus on adding two-digit and one-digit numbers (or multiples of 10) up to 100, with an emphasis on using place value strategies, objects, or drawings, and explaining thinking.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child connects counting with adding and subtracting, like counting on to add.
Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20 using strategies like counting on, making ten, breaking numbers apart, and using what they know about addition and subtraction.
Your child can break apart numbers up to five in more than one way.
Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20, using strategies like counting on, making ten, breaking numbers apart, and using related addition and subtraction facts.
Your child can use counting to help add or subtract numbers.
Your child can break apart and put together numbers up to 20 using tens and ones, showing it with objects, drawings, or equations.
Your child can switch the order of two numbers when adding and still find the same total, with numbers up to 20.
Your child can add two numbers with totals up to 20 and subtract using related facts.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Strategies within 20
coherence_reason: All standards focus on strategies for addition and subtraction within 20, including counting on, making ten, breaking apart numbers, and using related facts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands subtraction as finding the missing addend in an addition problem.
Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction problems with three whole numbers.
Your child can figure out what number you get when you take one away from another number.
Your child can figure out the missing number in addition or subtraction problems.
Your child understands that subtraction can be thought of as finding a missing addend in addition.
Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction problems where the unknown can be anywhere in the equation.
Your child can find the missing number in simple addition or subtraction problems with answers up to 10.
Your child can change a subtraction problem into an addition problem to find the missing number.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Finding the Missing Number
coherence_reason: All standards are about finding a missing number in addition or subtraction equations and understanding the relationship between the two operations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child works with addition and subtraction equations.
Add and subtract within 20.
Your child solves word problems with addition and subtraction within 20, using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.
Your child can show and solve problems using addition and subtraction.
Your child can show addition and subtraction by using objects, fingers, pictures, sounds, or by acting it out.
Your child can put together numbers up to five in different ways.
Your child can create and solve simple addition or subtraction story problems with numbers up to 20.
Your child can explain how they solve addition and subtraction problems up to 20 using words, objects, pictures, and number sentences.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Strategies and Word Problems within 20
coherence_reason: All standards focus on addition and subtraction within 20, including solving, representing, and explaining solutions using various strategies and contexts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve word problems that add three whole numbers with a total of 20 or less, using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.
Your child can solve word problems that add three whole numbers with a total of 20 or less.
Your child can solve word problems that add three numbers with a total of 20 or less.
Your child can choose numbers and plus signs from a word problem to write an addition sentence with three numbers adding up to 20 or less.
Your child uses what they know about addition facts to solve problems with three single-digit numbers.
Your child can add three whole numbers that total 20 or less.
Your child can solve word problems by adding three numbers up to 20, with or without using objects or models.
Your child can choose numbers and operation signs from a word problem to write a simple number sentence with a total of 20 or less.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Math: Addition — Word Problems with Three Numbers (Sum ≤ 20)
coherence_reason: All standards clearly focus on solving word problems by adding three numbers with sums up to 20, sometimes specifying use of objects, drawings, or equations.
